items.text: Chapter Five
Appearance and global spread of Tuta absoluta
5.1 Introduction

The story of the spread of Tuta absoluta represents one of the most rapid bioinvasions seen in modern agricultural pests. For many years, the insect remained associated with South America, then it appeared in Spain in 2006, and from there it spread within a short period to most regions of the Mediterranean basin, Europe, Africa, the Middle East, and Asia.
In less than two decades, the pest has transformed from a well-known regional problem in South American countries into a global threat to tomato production in open fields and protected crops.
Its spread was not the result of the insect's ability to fly alone, but rather resulted from the interaction of several factors, the most important of which are:

items: International trade in tomato fruits and plant materials.
Movement of seedlings, containers and means of transportation.
Continued cultivation of tomatoes and other solanaceous crops throughout the year.
The ability of an insect to produce successive generations.
Eggs and larvae hide in or on plant tissue.
Suitability of the climate of the Mediterranean, Africa and Asia for its establishment.
Delayed detection of infestation in new areas.
Poor agricultural hygiene procedures and crop waste disposal.

items.text: A distinction must be made between the insect’s actual introduction date and its first official record. The pest may reach an area and remain at low numbers for weeks or months before it is detected and diagnosed. The record years cited in this chapter therefore represent the first documented detection and do not always prove the exact date on which the first individual entered the country.
5.2 South America: original homeland
5.2.1 Geographic origin
Tuta absoluta is a pest of Neotropical origin, and its original habitat is located in South America, especially the western part of the Neotropical region, or what is known as:
Western Neotropics
Its presence has been historically linked to the areas extending on both sides of the Andes Mountains and tomato production areas in South American countries.
The pest has been recorded in its original habitat in countries including:

Therefore, the insect is not introduced or invasive within most regions of South America, but rather represents an indigenous part of the pest community associated with tomatoes and the Solanaceae family in the region.
5.2.2 First scientific description in Peru
British entomologist Edward Merrick described the insect scientifically in 1917, based on specimens collected in the Huancayo region of Peru.
When it was first described, it bore the name:
Phthorimaea absoluta Meyrick, 1917
Then its classification position changed several times, until it became known internationally as:
Tuta absoluta
Before it was recently returned to the genus Phthorimaea.

The description of the insect in 1917 does not mean that it appeared in Peru during that year, but rather it means that this is the date of its official scientific identification and granting it a taxonomic name.
5.2.3 Its spread within South America

During the twentieth century, the insect became one of the most important pests of tomatoes in South America. By the last decades of the twentieth century, it was registered in most of the major tomato-producing countries on the continent.
It helped spread it within South America:

items: Trading fruits and seedlings between regions.
The presence of potatoes, eggplant, and other solanaceous families.
The ability of butterflies to move between fields.
Persistence of volunteer plants and host weeds.

items.text: The pest has become a major problem in both fresh and industrial tomatoes, especially in Argentina, Brazil, Chile and Peru.
5.2.4 Environmental balance in the original habitat
In South America, the insect evolved for long periods within an environment containing a variety of biological enemies, including:
This is why Tuta absoluta is connected in its original habitat to a more complex ecological network than in the areas it has recently invaded.

But the presence of biological enemies did not prevent it from becoming an economic pest, especially when intensive spraying programs led to the killing of predators and parasites and the emergence of resistance to pesticides.
The accumulated experience in South America is an important basis for understanding:
5.3 Moving from South America to Europe
5.3.1 How did the insect leave its original habitat?
The route by which Tuta absoluta crossed from South America to Europe has not been conclusively settled.
Pest risk analyzes suggest that entry may have occurred through agricultural trade, particularly:

items: Fruits bearing eggs or larvae near the calyx.
Plant materials from the Solanaceae family.

items.text: Tuta absoluta moths have been found in supermarkets and European packing stations that receive imported fruit, demonstrating the ability of fruit trading chains to transmit the pest.

However, proving the presence of the insect in a filling station does not alone prove that this station was the source of the first spread. The exact route of entry into Spain therefore remained uncertain.
5.3.2 First record in Europe: Spain 2006
Tuta absoluta was first recorded in Europe at the end of 2006, in tomato fields in the Castellón district of the Valencia region in eastern Spain.

In 2007, the infestation appeared in other locations on the Spanish Mediterranean coast, especially in the province of Valencia. The pest was also discovered on the island of Ibiza in the Balearic Islands.
Some winter crops were severely infected, which made it clear early on that the pest can:

items: Causing large losses in a short time.

items.text: The Spanish record represented the fundamental turning point in the global history of the pest, as the Iberian Peninsula became the gateway for its spread to the Mediterranean basin and Afro-Eurasia.
5.3.3 Rapid spread within Spain
After the first discovery, the pest did not remain confined to Castellón, but rather moved to tomato-producing areas on the Spanish Mediterranean coast.
This helped:
Within a short period, the pest became present in most Spanish tomato-producing regions, including the Canary Islands.
5.4 Distribution of Tuta absoluta in Europe
5.4.1 Distribution in Western and Southern Europe
After being recorded in Spain, the insect quickly moved east and north through tomato production and marketing networks.
The most prominent stages of European expansion are:

items: Spain: First record at the end of 2006.
Italy: First recorded in spring 2008 in Calabria, then spread to multiple Italian regions.
Cyprus: Discovered in protected tomato production areas in 2009.
United Kingdom: The emergence of foci inside tomato production and trading facilities during 2009.
Germany, the Netherlands and other Nordic countries: records of individuals or localized foci, often associated with packing stations or protected cultivations.

items.text: Within a few years, the insect reached more than thirty countries within Europe and the western Antarctic region.
5.4.2 More than one propagation path
The spread of Tuta absoluta in Europe did not result entirely from a continuous advance of butterflies from Spain to neighboring countries.

In some countries geographically distant foci appeared at roughly the same time, as in Greece, where the nature of the distribution suggested the possibility of several separate introductions rather than a gradual, natural spread from a single foci.
This shows the existence of two overlapping paths:
Natural spread
It occurs through the flight of butterflies between adjacent areas and fields.
Human-assisted spread
It occurs through the transmission of:

items: Plant products between countries and regions.

items.text: This pattern explains the insect's appearance in locations far from the nearest known focus.
5.4.3 Southern Europe and Northern Europe
The chances of the pest becoming established were higher in southern European countries with a Mediterranean climate, where:

items: High density of fields and greenhouses.

items.text: In some northern European countries, low temperatures have limited their ability to continue in open fields throughout the year.
But heated greenhouses, packing plants and supermarkets provide environments in which the pest can survive or appear as temporary localized foci.
Therefore, the situation differed between European countries. The scourge was:

items: Well established and widespread in some countries.
Limited in certain regions of other countries.
Transient or subject to eradication in some cold regions.

items.text: 5.5 The insect's entry into Africa
5.5.1 North Africa in 2008
Tuta absoluta crossed the Mediterranean shortly after its appearance in Spain.
It was recorded during 2008 in three main countries of the Maghreb:
These records represented the beginning of the African invasion of the pest.

The geographical and commercial proximity between southern Europe and North Africa helped its movement, especially with the intensity of the tomato trade and the presence of connected production areas on the coasts of the Mediterranean.
After arriving in the Maghreb, it moved east towards Libya and Egypt, then south to regions of sub-Saharan Africa.
5.6 The entry of Tuta Absoluta into Egypt in 2009
5.6.1 First record in Egypt
The first documented observations of Tuta absoluta were recorded in Egypt in 2009.
Egyptian studies indicate that the first record occurred in Marsa Matrouh Governorate, located in northwestern Egypt near the Libyan border and the Mediterranean coast.
Then the pest appeared during 2010 in other regions, and then spread rapidly among tomato-producing governorates.
The database of the European and Mediterranean Organization for Plant Protection records the status of the pest in Egypt as follows:
5.6.2 Did the insect enter Egypt from Libya?
The geographical location of the first record in Marsa Matruh is consistent with the possibility of the pest moving from the West, after it spread in the Maghreb and Libya.
Transmission may have occurred through:

items: Movement of fruits and containers across the coast and borders.
Butterflies gradually move between agricultural areas.
More than one track at the same time.

items.text: But this geographical sequence does not represent conclusive evidence that Libya was the only direct source of the insect's introduction into Egypt.
There is not enough evidence to determine:

items: The shipment that transported the pest.
Date of arrival of the first individual.
Whether the entry occurred once or multiple times.

items.text: Therefore, it remains scientifically certain that the pest was discovered in Egypt in 2009, and then spread rapidly, while the direct entry route remains not definitively decided.
5.6.3 Reasons for its rapid spread within Egypt
Tuta absoluta found in the Egyptian agricultural environment very suitable conditions for settlement and reproduction.
Multiple tomato shoots
Tomatoes in Egypt are grown during multiple shifts and dates, providing plants suitable for the pest during most months of the year.
Transporting the crop between governorates
Fruits, seedlings, boxes, and means of transportation move between production areas, markets, packing stations, and factories, which increases the chances of transporting live stages.
Field ages overlap
Sometimes found in the same area:

items: Fields in the vegetative growth stage.
Fields whose harvest has ended and their residues have not been removed.

items.text: This overlap allows butterflies to constantly move from old to new fields.
Climatic conditions
Temperatures in large areas of Egypt allow the development of multiple generations, and protected crops and warm areas help activity continue during the winter.
Alternative families
Potatoes, eggplants and some nightshade weeds provide plants that can help the pest persist between tomato pods.
Poor agricultural hygiene
Leaving the plants after the end of the harvest results in the continuation of caterpillars and pupae and the emergence of a new generation of butterflies.
Early dependence on pesticides

The seriousness of the pest has prompted many producers to intensify spraying, but in some cases repeating the same pesticide combinations has led to decreased effectiveness and harm to biological enemies.
5.6.4 The impact of pest introduction on Egyptian tomato production
The emergence of Tuta absoluta has significantly changed tomato protection programs in Egypt.
Management became dependent on a set of procedures that were not applied with the same regularity before the pest entered, such as:

items: Consider pesticide residues and the pre-harvest period.

items.text: Control costs have also increased and crop success has become highly dependent on when the infestation is detected and the efficiency of the integrated programme.
5.7 Distribution from North Africa to Sub-Saharan Africa
5.7.1 Direction east and south
After the spread of the pest in North African countries, it moved through several paths:

items: From Egypt and Libya towards Sudan and East Africa.
From the Maghreb towards West Africa.
Through trade and transport networks between capitals and markets.
From tomato production centers to neighboring regions.

items.text: It was recorded in Sudan during 2010, and then continued to spread in the Horn of Africa and the east of the continent.
5.7.2 East Africa
The pest reached tomato producing areas in East African countries during the first half of the second decade of the twenty-first century.
Its presence was recorded in countries such as:
In Kenya, butterflies have been collected in pheromone traps since the beginning of 2014, and high numbers have appeared in some tomato-producing areas.
The movement of tomatoes between regions and markets, in addition to natural flight, helped in their rapid spread among East African countries.
5.7.3 West Africa
Tuta absoluta has also moved to West Africa, where it has appeared in tomato-growing areas in countries including:

Its spread in West Africa was linked to the movement of fruits across borders, the overlap of regional markets, the weakness of internal quarantine systems, and the presence of successive production seasons.
5.7.4 Central and Southern Africa
The pest continued to spread to:
It was recorded in Mozambique in 2016, then appeared in several provinces from north to south.
It was also recorded in South Africa during 2016–2017, completing a journey that extended from the northern coast of the continent to the far south.
By the end of the second decade of the twenty-first century, the pest had reached all major African regions during a period of approximately ten years since its first discovery in North Africa.
5.8 Spread in the Middle East
5.8.1 Türkiye is the gateway to Asian expansion
Tuta absoluta was recorded in Türkiye for the first time during August 2009, in the Urla district of Izmir Province on the Aegean coast.
The larvae showed:
The insect then spread rapidly throughout Turkish tomato-producing areas, and Türkiye became a major starting point for the pest's eastward spread into Asia.
5.8.2 The Levant
The pest appeared during the following period in a number of eastern Mediterranean countries, including:
The geographical and commercial interdependence between these countries, in addition to the movement of fruits and seedlings, helped the pest move between tomato production areas.
In Syria, butterflies and damage were observed in a number of coastal areas, and then the pest continued to move towards Iraq and the countries of the Arabian Peninsula.
5.8.3 Iraq
Tuta absoluta was recorded in Iraq for the first time during the fall of 2010, near the Rabia area in Nineveh Governorate adjacent to the Syrian border.
The record location reflects the possibility of its movement via the northwestern route from the Levant and Turkey.
Then the pest became recorded in other regions, especially with the movement of tomatoes between governorates and the availability of suitable climatic conditions.
5.8.4 Iran
The pest arrived in Iran at the beginning of the second decade of the twenty-first century, and then appeared in a number of tomato-producing regions.
Iran represented an important corridor for the pest’s movement towards:
The diversity of the Iranian climate and the presence of protected and open crops also helped to stabilize it in several regions.
5.8.5 Arabian Peninsula
Tuta absoluta continued to spread throughout the Arabian Peninsula and the Gulf, and was recorded in:
It was recorded in Qatar in 2012, and in the UAE in 2012 in tomato and eggplant greenhouses.
As for Yemen, it was discovered in January 2013, and then official surveys showed its spread in a large number of farms and governorates within a short period.
The rapid spread in dry environments indicates that lack of rain does not prevent the insect from settling when:
5.9 Distribution in Asia
5.9.1 From Türkiye to Central and South Asia
After the insect was recorded in Türkiye in 2009, it moved east along parallel paths:

items: Türkiye, the Levant, Iraq and Iran.

items.text: The spread within Asia has been rapid, especially in countries that grow tomatoes year-round or exchange fruits and seedlings across borders.
5.9.2 India
Tuta absoluta was recorded in India for the first time in 2014, in areas of the south and west of the country.
It then appeared in:
The large area of tomato cultivation, the multiple production seasons, and the movement of fruits between states helped expand the scope of the pest.
Its appearance in India became an important event in the course of the invasion because India represents a major center for tomato production and trade in South Asia.
5.9.3 Nepal
The insect was officially recorded in Nepal during May 2016 on a commercial tomato farm in the Kathmandu Valley.
The diagnosis is confirmed by:
Initial surveys showed that it was not confined to one farm, but had spread throughout the Kathmandu Valley and surrounding areas.
5.9.4 Pakistan, Bangladesh and Central Asia
The pest appeared during approximately the same period in other countries of South and Central Asia, including:
The location of these countries on land trade routes, along with the connectivity of tomato-producing areas, facilitated its spread.
It was recorded in Kyrgyzstan during the 2010s, and the first documented record appeared in Tajikistan shortly thereafter.
5.9.5 China
Tuta absoluta arrived in northwestern China during the second half of the 2010s.
The western border regions represented the first entry point for the pest, which is consistent with its movement through Central Asian countries.
Its arrival in China raised great concerns because:

items: The large area of tomato production.
The presence of large industrial tomato cultivations.
Possibility of moving from the western regions to the eastern production regions.

items.text: Climatic suitability models have shown that large areas of China and Asia may allow it to survive or evolve seasonally when the appropriate host and temperature are available.
5.10 Brief timeline of global diffusion

rows: Scientific description of the insect from samples collected in Peru
It has spread and established its status as a major pest of tomatoes in South America
It is widely recorded in a large number of South American countries
First European record in Castellón, Spain
It is spread on the Spanish coast and on the island of Ibiza
It was recorded in Morocco, Algeria, and Tunisia, and appeared in Italy and France
Registered in Egypt, Turkey and a large number of Mediterranean and European countries
It appears in Sudan, Syria, Iraq and several regions of the Middle East
Its expansion into East Africa, the Arabian Peninsula and Iran
Registered in India, Kenya and other African and Asian regions
It is distributed in Nepal, Pakistan, Bangladesh, Mozambique and sub-Saharan Africa
It reaches South Africa, parts of Central Asia and China
Continued local expansion and the emergence of new records in areas that were free or limited in infestation

items.text: This table presents selected milestones and is not a complete list for every country or first-record location.
5.11 Reasons for rapid global spread
5.11.1 International trade in tomatoes
Infected fruits can carry:

items: Pupae or larvae hiding in the accompanying detritus.
Butterflies inside packages or means of transportation.

items.text: The fruit may appear outwardly healthy even though there is a small larvae inside, especially at the beginning of the infestation.
Trade transports fruits within hours or days to distances far exceeding the natural flight range of the moth.
5.11.2 Movement of seedlings and plants
Infected seedlings pose a greater risk than fruit in some cases because it may carry:

items: Maidens between the leaves or in the planting medium.
A living plant that allows the pest cycle to be completed.

items.text: A limited number of infected seedlings can start a new focus within an empty area.
5.11.3 Packaging and transport boxes
Live stages may hide in:
The risk increases when containers are reused between farms and markets without cleaning.
5.11.4 Natural dispersal of butterflies
Adults can fly and move between fields, especially when:

items: High density inside the old field.
Availability of a fresh field nearby.
Wind activity in the direction of agricultural areas.

items.text: This ability is sufficient to expand the infestation locally between neighboring villages and regions, but it alone does not explain the sudden appearance of the pest in distant countries or islands.
5.11.5 Overlapping tomato growing seasons
The pest needs a plant host to continue reproduction. When tomatoes are grown all year round, the butterflies move from one button to another without any apparent interruption.
The persistence of host plants between seasons is known as:
Green Bridge — Green Bridge
This bridge may consist of:
5.11.6 Reproductive ability and multigenerationality
Tuta absoluta produces successive generations when heat and hosts are available.
Shorter generation times in warm conditions result in:

items: The pest moves from an unnoticed level to a severe outbreak.
Increased possibility of individuals moving with shipments.
Speed of colonization of new sites.
Overlapping of eggs, caterpillars, pupae and butterflies.

items.text: The pest does not depend on one generation per year, but can complete multiple generations depending on the climate.
5.11.7 Absence of forced rest
Tuta absoluta does not depend on forced winter dormancy that prevents it from being active when suitable conditions exist.
When tomatoes and the appropriate temperature are available inside the greenhouse or warm areas, the life cycle can continue during long periods of the year.
This feature helps:

items: Injury onset early in the following season.
Continuity of generations in tropical and subtropical regions.

items.text: 5.11.8 Hiding live stages
The pest is difficult to detect at the beginning of the invasion because:

items: Early tunnels are limited in size.
Larvae may be found inside the fruits.
Pupae may be found in soil or detritus.
Butterflies are small and hide inside the shoots.

items.text: The internal feeding nature of the insect sometimes allows it to pass through rapid inspections.
5.11.9 Similarity to other injuries
Initial Tuta injury may be confused with:

items: Mechanical wounds or rots in fruits.

items.text: Late diagnosis gives the pest enough time to produce one or more generations before containment measures are initiated.
5.11.10 Climate suitability
The conditions of large areas of the world are compatible with the thermal needs of the insect, especially:

items: Irrigated areas in the Middle East.

items.text: Extreme heat does not always mean pest elimination, because growing plants, greenhouses, and irrigation provide microclimates that are less harsh than the open air temperature.
5.11.11 Expanding the range of families
Tomatoes are the main economic host, but the pest can survive on other plants, especially from the Solanaceae family.
The hosts or related plants include:

items: Some types of pepper in specific conditions.

items.text: The presence of these plants prevents a complete interruption of the pest cycle between tomato seasons.
5.11.12 Delayed quarantine and monitoring procedures
When the pest appeared in new areas, many producers or inspectors were not sufficiently aware of its symptoms.
This led to:

items: Failure to dispose of field waste.
The focus expanded before it was officially announced.

items.text: Once the pest spreads over a large area, complete eradication becomes very difficult.
5.11.13 Pesticide resistance
Pesticide resistance does not represent a geographic route of transmission for the insect, but it does increase its ability to establish after arrival.
When an insect community with degrees of resistance enters an area that relies on the same pesticides, initial treatments may fail to eliminate it.
Intensive spraying after discovering the pest also leads to:

items: The presence of foci capable of spreading.

items.text: Therefore, imposing chemical spraying alone is not sufficient to prevent the expansion of the pest.
5.12 Natural spread and human spread
The Tuta Absoluta movement can be divided into two levels:
Distribution is short and medium distance
It is mainly caused by moths, and is transmitted between:
Long-distance propagation
Mainly associated with human activity, especially:

The combination of the two patterns explains the great speed of the global invasion; Trade moves the pest to a distant focus, and then adult moths and local crop movements spread it into the new area.
5.13 Why is it difficult to eradicate after stabilization?
A small focus can be eradicated if detected early and isolated, but eradication becomes difficult after the pest has reached several farms.
This is due to:

items: Multiple phases present at the same time.
The larvae hide inside the plant.
The presence of pupae in soil and waste.
Difficulty coordinating control among all farmers.
The ability of a small number of individuals to establish a new society.
Resistance of some populations to pesticides.

items.text: When the pest becomes widespread, the goal of eradication shifts to:

items: Preventing its movement to new areas.

items.text: 5.14 Lessons learned from global deployment
Monitoring should precede the appearance of damage
When fruit damage appears on a large scale, the pest is already established. Monitoring should therefore begin with traps and plant inspection before losses appear.
The nursery is part of the protection system
An infected seedling is able to transmit the pest to a new field, even if it is far from the nearest known outpost.
Waste management is a regional procedure
Leaving an infected field after harvest does not only harm its owner, but also turns it into a source of butterflies that attack the surrounding fields.
Administrative boundaries do not stop the pest
Fruits and butterflies move between centers, governorates, and countries, and therefore management requires coordination between producers and regulatory authorities.
The correct name is necessary
Confusion between Tuta and Leaf Miner delays diagnosis and leads to the use of inappropriate monitoring and control methods.
Spraying alone does not prevent spread

The history of the invasion has proven that reliance on pesticides did not stop the expansion of the pest, but rather led in many areas to the emergence of resistance and damage to biological enemies.
5.15 Chapter summary
Tuta absoluta originated in South America, was scientifically described from Peru in 1917, and then became a major pest of tomatoes in a large number of countries on the continent.
The global invasion phase began when it was discovered in Spain at the end of 2006. It then spread rapidly throughout southern Europe and the Mediterranean basin.

It arrived in North Africa in 2008, where it was recorded in Morocco, Algeria, and Tunisia, then appeared in Egypt in 2009. Egyptian studies indicate that the first record occurred in Marsa Matruh, before it spread during the following years in most Egyptian tomato-producing areas.
In the same year, the pest was recorded in Türkiye, from where its spread spread to the Levant, Iraq, Iran, and the Arabian Peninsula, and then to South, Central, and East Asia.
Within about ten years of its entry into Africa, it reached all the main regions of the continent, and it also continued to expand within India, Nepal, Pakistan, Central Asia, and China.
This spread did not result from a single factor, but rather from an interaction:

items: Difficulty in eradication after the formation of several foci.

items.text: This trip demonstrates that management of Tuta absoluta does not begin with spraying the infected field, but rather begins with preventing its transmission, examining seedlings and fruits, setting traps early, removing crop residues, and coordinating control between fields and regions.
References
